claim_text: The Affordable Care Act (ACA) has resulted in millions of Californians having comprehensive, affordable health coverage.
The ACA does not discriminate against people with pre-existing conditions.
1 in 6 Californians benefit from the Affordable Care Act (ACA).
Repealing and replacing the Affordable Care Act with Trumpcare could jeopardize healthcare for 5 million Californians.
A recent Carnegie Mellon study indicated that children are 50 times more likely than adults to have their identities stolen.
Prior to the enactment of AB 1580, parents had to go through a rigorous process to freeze their children’s credit report.
The process to freeze a child’s credit report previously included a 60-90 day wait period and the provision of notarized identification documents to credit bureaus.
The new Child Identity Theft Law allows parents, legal guardians, or conservators to freeze the credit of children and incapacitated adults.
The new law allows for the credit freezing process to be significantly shortened.
AB 1665 aims to expand broadband access and digital literacy in communities deprived of reliable internet connections.
The California Advanced Services Fund (CASF) is designed to incentivize the expansion of broadband infrastructure to 98% of California households.
AB 1665 expands the CASF goal to 98% of households in every geographic region of California.
AB 1665 will focus broadband infrastructure projects in rural California to ensure access in underserved areas.
Internet access has become essential for health, economic, and educational opportunities.
Investment in internet access correlates with investment in education, healthcare access, and economic development.
Assembly Bill 669 reauthorizes the California Department of Transportation to test Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control technologies.
CACC technologies allow trucks to safely follow each other with less than 100 feet between vehicles by syncing acceleration and braking.
Platooning enhances safety and lessens traffic.
CACC technologies can reduce fuel consumption by 10 to 15 percent.
Platooning could lead to 4.5% fuel savings for the lead truck and 10% fuel savings for the following truck.
The goods movement sector accounts for half the state’s diesel consumption.
Fuel savings from CACC technologies will have a significant, positive impact on air quality.
The Urban Greening Program is financed by Cap-and-Trade revenue.
The Urban Greening Program focuses on projects that reduce greenhouse gases.
The Urban Greening Program is aimed at decreasing energy consumption.
The Urban Greening Program provides other environmental benefits.
The projects approved will create job opportunities.
The approved projects will take place in areas where constituents feel left behind.
High-interest rates on loans above $2,500 are deemed 'unconscionable' by the California Supreme Court.
The ruling in De La Torre v. CashCall found that interest rates on consumer loans of $2,500 or more can render the loans unconscionable and void.
The California Legislature failed to pass AB 2500, which aimed to extend the interest rate cap for loans up to $5,000.
Predatory lenders deliberately target Latino and African American borrowers by establishing stores in minority and low-income communities.
According to a 2016 report by the California Department of Business Oversight (DBO), 58% of installment loans of $2,500 to $5,000 had APRs of 100% or higher.
California experienced the most devastating wildfire in state history in October 2017.
44 Californian lives were claimed due to the October 2017 wildfires.
Over 14,700 homes and 728 businesses were damaged or destroyed during the October 2017 wildfires.
The total damage from the October 2017 wildfires exceeded $12 billion.
Wildfires of significant magnitude pollute the air and negate gains made in reducing California’s carbon footprint.
AB 1772 extends the time homeowners have to rebuild insured properties from 2 to 3 years after a declared wildfire emergency.
Extending the time for consumers to recoup their losses after a fire will minimize stressful battles with insurance companies.
AB 2380 requires the state to develop standards and regulations for privately contracted fire personnel during active fire emergencies.
The presence of privately contracted fire personnel in evacuated zones risks their safety and could require public personnel to rescue them.
The increase of wildfires has put more members of the public at risk than ever before.
AB 2518 requires the Department of Forestry and Fire Protection to explore markets for innovative forest products and mass timber.
Overly dense forests are a major contributor to the state’s wildfires.
Finding uses for wood from overly dense forests will promote environmentally safe and clean uses for forest waste.
Incentivizing private investment in forest health is necessary to protect natural resources and public health and safety.
Californians are at unprecedented risk of wildfire disaster due to the current state of the forests.
The existing wood products market must be expanded to include innovative timber products that utilize small diameter trees.
AB 324 will provide expanded childcare workforce development grants to new providers.
The bill aims to increase the state's capacity to administer a higher level of care to an increased number of children.
Training and retaining qualified, committed professionals is critical to children's future.
AB 324 requires the California Department of Education to develop guidelines for a standardized, effective, and organized professional development and retention system for childcare professionals.
The guidelines expand eligibility in the program and link stipends to a teacher’s higher education attainment plan.
AB 324 aims to ensure that stipend amounts are sufficient to recruit and retain a high-quality, diverse childcare education workforce.
Supporting the childcare workforce is expected to help prevent the achievement gap.
Investing in the childcare workforce is anticipated to lead to more jobs and better outcomes for youth.
The legislation aims to provide new support for parents, which may contribute to stronger families.
Governor Newsom's plan to tackle the homelessness crisis includes funding devoted to housing and supportive services such as mental health and addiction.
Governor Newsom's request for more transparency in school systems aims to prevent social bullying and aid homeless youth.
Budgetary funding to incentivize housing construction will benefit workers and families looking for affordable housing.
Governor Newsom's dedication to boost apprenticeships and training is intended to foster a skilled workforce for the evolving economy.
Governor Newsom's plan to expand the California Earned Income Tax Credit will put money back into the wallets of working people and stimulate the economy.
Micro-irrigation decreases water usage on farms by up to 60%.
Micro-irrigation increases crop yields by 90%.
Micro-irrigation significantly reduces water consumption in the state.
Micro-irrigation reduces pesticides water-run off that contaminates state drinking water.
California's goal is to rely on 100% clean energy sources by 2045.
Only 9% of California’s low-income infants and toddlers who are income-eligible for subsidized child care are enrolled.
Fewer families have access to quality early learning programs with comprehensive support services that research shows has the greatest positive impact on low-income babies.
The first three years are critical to a child's development, providing the platform for future health and well-being.
High-quality early learning programs and support are needed before kindergarten or preschool to benefit children.
Strengthening and expanding child care programs based on the effective, high-quality Early Head Start model can give more children a better start in life.
AB 167 aims to improve quality standards for child care programs based on the proven Early Head Start model.
AB 167 seeks to expand access to high-quality, full-day, full-year child care for infants and toddlers in high-need communities.
AB 275 will require state agencies with 30 or more employees to designate one or more liaisons to engage with Native American tribes.

AB 275 updates current law regarding the possession of California Native American human remains and funerary objects for repatriation, requiring agencies and museums to consider 'tribal traditional knowledge' with comparable or greater weight than nontribal sources of information.
The California Native American Heritage Commission (NAHC) was created in 1976 to identify, catalog, and protect Native American cultural resources.
The NAHC's current Five-Year Strategic Plan identified several areas for improvement, which AB 275 seeks to address.
AB 1128 will help more seniors remain in their homes and in communities by streamlining the licensing for PACE facilities.
AB 1128 will facilitate statewide growth of PACE programs, enabling more seniors to access these services and age in place.
Reducing state-associated costs with nursing home care is a potential outcome of AB 1128.
PACE is a well-respected, efficient program that aims to keep seniors in their homes and communities rather than institutions.
AB 1128 will benefit thousands of elderly Californians by accelerating and streamlining the growth of PACE programs.
PACE is a proven model of providing care to frail elderly individuals that allows them to live in the community.
PACE programs coordinate and deliver preventative and long-term care services to elderly individuals who would otherwise be in nursing homes.
PACE is the only provider-based model of care where a single entity is entirely responsible for the delivery, outcomes, and cost of care.
Eligible beneficiaries of PACE must be 55 years or older and state certified to qualify for nursing home care.
Program recipients of PACE receive all of their services through a one-stop-shop PACE center while still residing in their community.
There are currently 12 PACE programs providing essential care to 8,000 seniors operating in 42 sites across California.
California consumers are served best when professional licensing laws balance regulatory efficiency with public protection.
The Accountability Committee plans to focus future hearings on issues identified in correspondence with constituents.
The oversight hearings are a critical opportunity for the Committee to study the implementation, operation, and effectiveness of state programs and agencies.
The state’s accounting, budget, cash management, and procurement information technology system is referred to as FI$Cal.
AB 1767 will expand school district suicide prevention policies to be age appropriate for kindergarten and grades 1 to 6.
The policy will be developed by school and community stakeholders, school-employed mental health professionals, and suicide prevention experts.
The 40th Assembly District has experienced occurrences of suicide among its young residents.
At the beginning of the 2018 school year, 4 students died by suicide in the first two weeks of the school.
Assembly Bill 467 requires equal prize compensation for all athletes, regardless of gender, as a condition for approving a lease or permit request for any sporting event held on state lands.
Equal pay disparities exist in almost every major sport.
The passing of AB 467 is a societal shift toward equity and progress.
The implementation of AB 467 is projected to set precedence for women everywhere.
California has the highest rate of homelessness in the nation.
Since 2017, homelessness is up by forty-two percent in Orange County.
An array of innovative strategies is needed to resolve the homelessness crisis.
The California Department of Housing and Community Development is responsible for reviewing and approving ordinances developed by counties or cities.
AB 143 would create flexibility to expedite the creation of shelters and permanent supportive housing.
AB 143 expands emergency housing to include homeless shelters and permanent supportive housing.
Assembly Bill 690 will increase access to pharmacy care in vulnerable and rural areas of California by authorizing the use of telepharmacy.
Telepharmacy is a proven model to provide enhanced care that is both safe and effective.
The use of telepharmacy technology was legalized in California for the first time by Assemblymember Aguiar-Curry's AB 401 in 2017.
The Board of Pharmacy had been unable to implement the telepharmacy law passed in 2017.
Telepharmacy still has not been used in California despite the law passed two years ago.
AB 690 codifies the qualifications required for pharmacy technicians to work in remote dispensing pharmacies.
The telepharmacy law will provide access to better patient education.
The telepharmacy law will make it easier for patients to follow schedules for their medications.
The telepharmacy law will result in better health outcomes for Californians.
AB 690 streamlines the process for pharmacies that are destroyed or damaged in natural disasters to relocate and reopen.
Pharmacists whose businesses are destroyed during a disaster currently must completely re-apply for a pharmacy license to reopen.
AB 690 allows impacted pharmacies to quickly relocate and reopen following an emergency.
Continuity in pharmacy care is critical for patients following a disaster.
AB 2002 would mandate state contracts involving tropical forest-risk commodities to only go to companies with responsible practices.
The world’s rainforests are in crisis.
Tropical forests cover roughly seven percent of the Earth’s surface but harbor close to half of all species on Earth.
An estimated 18 million acres of forest are lost every year, largely due to the expansion of agribusiness plantations.
Tropical deforestation and related land-use changes are responsible for nearly a quarter of global carbon dioxide emissions.
Tropical forests are essential for stabilizing the global climate.
The rapid loss of tropical forests is a crisis of epic proportions.
The sea level has already risen eight inches along the San Francisco coast over the last century.
Sea level is expected to climb another 1 to 3.4 feet by 2100.
Sea level rise is one of the most serious consequences of climate change.
San Mateo County has more people and property value at risk from rising sea levels than any other county in California.
San Mateo County has created the first county-level agency in California to address sea level rise.
Local jurisdictions would benefit from working together on a regional basis to address the interrelated impacts of sea level rise.
The recent LAO report on sea level rise calls for state actions to support, fund, and accelerate local, collaborative efforts.

The Integrated School-Based Behavioral Health Partnership Program encourages local educational agencies and county behavioral health agencies to collaborate on providing mental and behavioral health services on school campuses.
Assembly Bill 2265 authorizes counties to treat patients with mental health issues and co-occurring substance use disorders statewide under the Mental Health Services Act.
Legislative efforts are being made to address homelessness, mental health, and education during the pandemic.
The illegal cannabis market accounts for as much as 80% of cannabis sales.
The underground cannabis market presents public safety and health threats.
Cannabis products sold in the illegal market do not meet established standards.
Cannabis products sold illegally evade state testing and put people at risk.
Civil penalties for aiding unlicensed cannabis activity will encourage operators to join the legal market.
The illicit cannabis market contributes to dwindling tax revenues in California.
Fracking is one of the most toxic and environmentally damaging forms of fossil fuel extraction.
Fracking pollutes water sources and air, and can even cause earthquakes.
Burning fossil fuel is at the heart of the problem of carbon emissions.
California is one of the largest oil producers.
Fracking can have serious environmental and community health consequences.
Fracking impacts water supply, air quality, and seismic activity.
Many California communities rely on scant water resources, which are becoming scarcer due to climate change.
ICE has a shameful record of human rights abuses, including subjecting immigrants detained in their facilities to inhumane conditions that have resulted in COVID outbreaks and COVID deaths.
ICE agents have posed as local law enforcement even though they are not trained or deputized as police officers.
ICE has misrepresented administrative warrants as judicial warrants.
The agency has failed miserably in controlling for and treating the spread of COVID-19 in detention facilities.
Little to no medical care is provided in some detention cases.
Thousands of children have been removed from their parents for no reason other than to implement a 'zero-tolerance' policy.
ICE has engaged in scapegoating and fear mongering while denying immigrants their basic dignity and humanity.
AB 378 seeks to update gender-specific code sections relating to the offices of California constitutional officers.
Current language in California law that assumes constitutional officers are male plays into sexist bias.
Gender-neutral language reflects that anyone, regardless of gender identity, can serve at the highest levels of California government.
The majority of California’s constitutional offices are held by women.
California Government Code currently refers to some office holders using male pronouns.
Words used in legislation matter and should reflect the representation of all genders.
Diversity and inclusion should be reflected at all levels of state government.
California’s Government Code is a reflection of the state's values.
Gender-neutral language will reflect a modern understanding of representation.
AB 378 eliminates gendered language in reference to government officers and members of the public.
The total cost of attending college, including food, housing, and health care, cripples students and undermines their ability to succeed.
Many students are struggling to make ends meet, working multiple jobs and relying on food pantries.
Students' needs for food, housing, and mental health care are crucial for their success in higher education.
California's higher education system must respond to issues that were never previously envisioned.
The Master Plan for California higher education needs to be updated to adequately prepare all students for success.
A good quality e-bike can cost between $600 and $10,000.
Steep costs of e-bikes prevent many lower income Californians from switching to this low-maintenance, zero-emission alternative for short trips.
Electric bikes can accomplish almost all of the trips that electric cars can, including taking children to school and hauling heavy loads.
California lags behind world leaders in the adoption of electric bike technology.
More than fifteen percent of vehicle trips in California are less than one mile.
Eighty percent of vehicle trips in California are less than ten miles.
Nearly half (46 percent) of e-bike trips replace car commutes, at an average of 9.3 miles per trip.
California is gearing up to invest an additional $1.5 billion to promote electric vehicle purchases and bolster charging infrastructure.
The Clean Cars 4 All program incentivizes trade-ins for credit toward the purchase of hybrid and zero-emission vehicles, including e-bikes.
There are equity gaps in California's current approach to incentivizing electric bike purchases.
Nearly 3,800 hate incidents have been reported across the country since the COVID-19 pandemic began, according to StopAAPIHate.com.
The U.S. Department of Justice estimates over half of all hate crimes are never reported.
A toll-free hate crimes hotline and an online reporting system will allow victims and witnesses to report hate incidents safely and anonymously.
Victims of hate crimes may face language or cultural barriers or be undocumented, which complicates their ability to report incidents.
The California Department of Justice should serve as the central clearinghouse for hate crime reports to coordinate with local law enforcement.
A centralized, statewide approach to track hate crimes is expected to assist victims and make California communities safer.
There has been a skyrocketing increase in hate incidents targeting Asian and Pacific Islander communities during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Hate crimes targeting Asian Americans in America’s largest cities have increased 164% in the past year.
The Anti-Defamation League has reported a surge of anti-Jewish incidents in recent weeks, including violent attacks in Los Angeles and other major urban areas.
Law enforcement agencies in California routinely fail to adequately identify, report, or respond to hate crimes.
Inadequate policies and lack of oversight by the California Department of Justice contributed to a systemic under-reporting of hate crimes across the state.
Hate crimes in the City of Los Angeles increased 40% from 2016 to 2019.
Data collected by the U.S. Department of Justice suggests that hate crimes occur 24 to 28 times more frequently than they are reported.
More than 70% of Asian Americans in California live in fear of racially motivated violence and hate crimes.
Breathing in toxic smoke has been linked to increased rates of chronic heart and respiratory diseases.
Without adequate protective equipment, farmworkers risk lifelong debilitating illnesses.
AB 73 will ensure a dedicated stockpile of N95 respirators for farmworkers.
Cal/OSHA strike teams will be deployed to ensure employers are in compliance with Cal/OSHA regulations during major unhealthy air quality events.
Climate change is causing the worst fires in California's history.
AB 73 requires Cal/OSHA to develop and distribute information on wildfire safety to agricultural employees in Spanish and English, as well as via pictograms.
The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need for teachers that are trained in computer science education.
Technology-related challenges posed a significant barrier to students’ education during the transition to remote learning.
The Computer Science Access Initiative awards grants to support and encourage the preparation of credentialed teachers to earn a supplementary authorization in computer science.
Grants will be prioritized for teachers who work in school districts serving a higher proportion of low-income and underserved students.
The need for computer science as part of primary education for all students is paramount.
California's high schools offer few computer science courses despite a booming tech sector.
The Computer Science Access Initiative funding demonstrates the state's commitment to investing in computer science education.
Ghost guns are showing up at crime scenes across the country.
Ghost guns do not require a background check.
AB 1057 will help protect communities from deadly DIY weapons.
There has been a surge in gun sales since the onset of the pandemic.
Ghost guns are more difficult to track compared to conventional firearms.
Businesses selling ghost gun kits are experiencing shipping delays due to high demand.
AB 1057 ensures that known dangers cannot possess precursor parts that can be converted into functional firearms.
The presence of firearms in domestic violence situations significantly increases the risk of homicide.
Ghost guns have quickly become a significant issue in gun safety.
California's Gun Violence Restraining Order (GVRO) law has received broad bipartisan support.
Dangerous individuals should not have access to deadly weapons in emergency situations.
Gun Violence Restraining Orders can prevent mass shootings and save lives.
Individuals prohibited from possessing purchased firearms should also be prohibited from possessing homemade firearms.
The increase in gun violence has become a national crisis.
Only 19 percent of students with a transfer goal successfully transfer within four years.
Only 28 percent of students with a transfer goal transfer within six years.
AB 928 addresses persistent community college transfer rates by increasing coordination between California’s public colleges and universities.
AB 928 aims to eliminate unnecessary hurdles that keep students from transferring.
AB 928 establishes a committee to provide coordination between California’s higher education systems.
AB 928 requires the creation of a single lower division general education pathway that meets transfer admission requirements to both CSU and UC.
AB 928 ensures every community college student wanting to transfer is placed on the Associate Degree for Transfer pathway.
AB 1111 requires all California Community Colleges to adopt a common course numbering system for all general education requirement courses and transfer pathway courses.
The State Budget includes $10 million to fund the creation of a common course numbering system.
Assembly Bill 1993 would mandate all businesses in California to require their employees and independent contractors to receive the COVID-19 vaccine.
COVID-19 vaccines are safe and effective at preventing most from experiencing serious illness, hospitalization, and death.
Vaccines help reduce the spread of the COVID-19 virus in communities, thereby protecting the most vulnerable.
Vaccine mandates work to increase vaccination rates.
When people are vaccinated, the world is safer.
Vaccines and mandates can help businesses prosper.
The U.S. accounts for 4% of the world’s population but 21% of the world’s COVID cases and 15% of the world’s COVID deaths.
California has an opportunity to improve public safety by mandating vaccines in workplaces.
Coronavirus variants continue to disrupt business operations and create financial uncertainty for small businesses.
Businesses are looking for a common statewide standard for public safety to operate safely and predictably.
Employees deserve safe workplaces, and vaccines are key to keeping individuals and communities safe.
Fewer than 60 percent of eligible voters have exercised their right to vote in each statewide election in California since 1972.
Approximately three million eligible voters in California were not registered in the most recent election in 2020.
Unregistered eligible voters are disproportionately the most marginalized members of communities.
Confusion hampers engagement for roughly 50,000 formerly incarcerated people whose voting rights were restored by Proposition 17 in 2020.
AB 2576 creates the Civic Learning, Outreach, and Engagement Fund for the Secretary of State.
AB 2576 offers counties with low voter registration rates the opportunity to conduct outreach and education catered to their communities' unique needs.
The bill AB 2576 will increase civic education and engagement for young people.
California’s adoption of the State Seal of Civic Engagement in schools offers a unique opportunity for partnerships between Registrars of Voters and educators.
Increased voter participation among future voters can result from innovative partnerships fostered by the State Seal program.
The vibrancy and safety of democracy improve with increased participation from the public in choosing their representatives.
Foreign investments in US companies have skyrocketed in recent years.
In 1982, foreign investors owned about five percent of all U.S. corporate equity.
By 2019, foreign investors owned 40% of U.S. equity including foreign governments.
Governments throughout the world own substantial positions in politically active U.S. companies.
Norges Bank holds on average 1.4% of the US’s publicly traded companies.
Saudi Arabia plans to invest about $10 billion into companies, more than doubling its assets by 2025.
Federal law does not address the issue of political spending by U.S. corporations that are partially owned by foreign investors.
The US Supreme Court’s 2010 ruling in Citizens United v. FEC created a massive loophole for foreign interests to influence U.S. corporations.
AB 1819 would close the loophole created by the Citizens United ruling.
A corporation would be deemed 'foreign influenced' if one percent of shares are owned by a single foreign investor.
A corporation would be deemed 'foreign influenced' if five percent of shares are owned by multiple foreign investors.
A corporation would be deemed 'foreign influenced' if a foreign entity participates in decision-making with respect to state or local political spending.
US election law is very clear that there should be zero foreign money in US elections.
Gun shows are a source for illegally trafficked firearms.
Gun shows have been criticized as being the critical moment in the chain of custody for many guns.
Gun violence is among the greatest public health crises facing children and youth.
Nearly 7,000 children younger than 18 are killed or wounded by gunshots each year.
Firearm-related deaths are now the leading cause of death for children ages 1 to 19.
The United States experiences far more gun violence per person than virtually every other modern industrialized country.
The United States has one of the most pervasive gun cultures in the world supported by a powerful gun lobby.
The legislation will make the Ventura Fairgrounds and surrounding communities safer from gun violence.
Sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) is 80 percent cleaner than traditional jet fuel.
AB 1322 aims to help achieve California’s goal for a net-zero emission economy by 2045.
California's policies have historically incubated new technologies that change the world and combat climate change.
Clean solar and wind power are now the least expensive forms of new energy because of California.
Aviation is a growing source of emissions that contribute to air pollution and climate change.
California aims to develop and implement a strategy for reaching a 20% sustainable aviation fuel target by 2030.
The City of Newark aims to implement its first Broadband Master Plan.
The funding will allow Newark to develop an inventory of existing fiber optic infrastructure and identify underserved areas.
Piedmont Middle School's multipurpose space has not been upgraded since its construction in 1959.
The new facility at Piedmont Middle School will include a full-size middle school basketball court.
The Teen Center at Lake Elizabeth in Fremont provides unique facilities for meetings, parties, and special events.
The Fremont Teen Center improvements will enhance its safety and capacity to serve youth.
The I-680 Sabercat Bridge & Trail will serve as a landmark bicycle and pedestrian gateway in Fremont.
The Fremont Housing Navigation Center opened in 2020 to support unhoused residents.
Participants in the Housing Navigation Center receive wrap-around services including health and wellness resources.
The Milpitas Homelessness Task Force prioritizes projects to address homelessness in Milpitas.
Milpitas plans to enhance its bicycle facilities to promote a multi-modal roadway environment.
The All-Inclusive Playground project in Santa Clara's Central Park will serve residents of all ages and abilities.
The All-Inclusive Playground project will include ADA renovations.
Hate crimes have increased by 89% over the past decade in California.
The California Nonprofit Security Grant Program was established in 2019 in response to the deadly shooting at Chabad of Poway.
The California state budget included a $50 million allocation for the Nonprofit Security Grant Program this year.
California has funded a total of $110 million in anti-hate programs.
AB 1664 will create more flexibility for how grant funding may be used, including for security training.
Some non-profit organizations in California, including abortion providers, face vandalism and threats of violence because of their mission-driven work.
Asian Americans, particularly women and the elderly, have been targeted by a staggering increase in hate, bias, and violence since the start of the pandemic.
There is a clear need to extend and expand the California State Nonprofit Security Grant Program to better protect vulnerable communities.
Loma Linda University Children’s Hospital received $4 million in the 2022-23 state budget for a program to offer individualized physical and mental care to abused foster children.

The hospital's budget request included expectations to see children with abusive head trauma, inflicted burns, fractures, abdominal injury, child sexual assault, medical child abuse, and severe neglect.
The Resiliency Institute for Childhood Adversity program will provide continuity of care for vulnerable and transient foster youth.
The clinic will offer individualized, consistent care to foster children who have suffered abuse, aimed at mitigating and healing physical and emotional trauma.
The funding will enable continued mental health and physical care for youth who often fall through the cracks of the healthcare system.
The governor signed AB 2317 to create, license and regulate psychiatric crisis residential treatment for children and youth.
Tolls can be incredibly cost-prohibitive throughout the Bay Area and Southern California.
The financial burden of toll roads can lead to significant economic impacts on individuals who must use them.
Disabled veterans may find it more difficult to attain a job or seek ongoing medical care due to financial obstacles.
Nearly three in ten disabled veterans report that their impairments have prevented them from getting or keeping a job.
Two-thirds of disabled veterans who are 100% disabled say their impairments have prevented them from obtaining a job.
The signing of AB 2949 will provide financial relief to veterans by exempting them from toll costs.
Floating offshore wind on the West Coast is necessary to address the climate crisis.
Floating offshore wind is economically viable for the companies that won the recent auction.
The Bureau of Ocean Energy Management conducted its first-ever auction of lease areas for offshore wind projects.
Assembly Bill 972 extends California's prohibition on distributing deepfakes and manipulated media of political candidates within 60 days of an election.
Assembly Bill 775 bans political candidates or committees from pre-checking the recurring donation box on their websites and requires affirmative consent for recurring contributions.
The Cybersecurity Regional Alliances and Multistakeholder Partnerships Pilot Program aims to address the immediate cybersecurity workforce gap.
The $4 million appropriation for the cybersecurity program is intended to increase the pipeline of students pursuing cybersecurity careers.
Data from the National Youth in Transition Database indicates that youth in the foster care system are five times more likely to abuse drugs than their peers.
According to the Administration for Children and Families, 27% of foster youth aged 17 have been referred for substance abuse treatment.
Opioid blocker nasal sprays can completely reverse a fentanyl overdose in 30 seconds.
In 2021, opioid deaths in California skyrocketed to 6800 due to the availability of the synthetic opioid fentanyl.
One out of every five California youths aged 15 to 24 who died in 2021 were killed by a fentanyl overdose.
The California Department of Justice has seized over 4 million fentanyl pills and almost 900 pounds of fentanyl powder since last spring.
Opioid blockers bind to the same receptors in the brain used by opioids and reverse or block the effects of the drug.
Opioid blocker nasal sprays have no side effects and are safe to use even if the person is experiencing a medical issue unrelated to an overdose.
The effectiveness of opioid blockers requires that someone near the person overdosing has access to the medication.
AB 24 focuses on putting opioid blocker nasal spray into the hands of employees in public locations where people are likely to overdose.
Locations that do not keep opioid blockers in accessible places can be fined up to 1000 dollars.
Fentanyl is becoming cheaper and more accessible than opioid blockers.
Disenfranchisement of incarcerated people does nothing to improve the safety of our communities or encourage rehabilitation.
All the data shows us that voting reduces recidivism.
93% of currently incarcerated respondents expressed that they want to vote in order to contribute positively to the community.

Voting creates an opportunity for people in prison to think about their communities positively as they prepare to reenter society.

When 80% of people incarcerated in California prisons are people of color, denying these individuals the right to vote perpetuates the root causes that led to mass incarceration.
Systemic racism and voter suppression are longstanding symptoms of anti-Black hate.

The opportunity exists to draw commercial debt and equity investments from the private and philanthropic sectors to develop and acquire affordable housing for transition-aged foster youth.

Chief Sean Washington developed one of the nation's first electric patrol vehicle programs.

Data brokers collect, analyze, and sell personal information about consumers, aggregating data from public records, social media platforms, online transactions, and much more to create detailed profiles on millions of people.
Data brokers have to register with the California Attorney General, but they don’t have to report what kinds of information they collect and sell.
Under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), individuals have the right to require a data broker to delete information they collected directly from them.
Individuals cannot require a broker to delete information they may have acquired about them from other sources.
SB 362 would require data brokers to register with the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) and disclose the types of personal information they collect.
The CPPA would create a simple way for Californians to direct all data brokers to delete their personal information, free of charge.
Data brokers that fail to adhere to the law would face civil penalties and administrative fines set by the Attorney General and the CPPA.
